data.title: The Case For Taylor Morrison In The Builder Bearish To Bullish Reversal
data.description:
When I last wrote about Taylor Morrison Homes (NYSE:TMHC) almost a year and a half ago, I called the stock “fairly priced” given mild guidance and a strong rally from the lows at that point. I posited the stock in the short-term could go up another 50% just on speculation. However, I was unwilling to bet on such an outcome and left my rating at hold. TMHC went on to gain another 27% over the next 8 months before topping out at its last all-time high. I finally upgraded TMHC to a buy last month as part of a tactical case for buying home builders as part of a bearish to bullish reversal featuring housing market news moving from awful to “just” bad. In TMHC’s favor at the time was being one of the few home builders enjoying a bullish breakout above its 200-day moving average (DMA) (the blue line below). While the market responded poorly to a subsequent Q2 earnings report, I am now making the fundamental case for TMHC as my leading home builder for the bearish to bullish turn.

Relatively Encouraging Results

Taylor Morrison provided upside surprises and met guidance on some key metrics for Q2. The following list compares previous guidance for Q2 to actual results (expected vs actual).

  • Home closings: ~3,200 vs 3,340 (+4%)
  • Average closing price: ~$585,000 vs $589,000 (+0.7%)
  • Home closings gross margin: ~23% vs 23.0% (flat)
  • Ending active community count: ~345 vs 345 (0%)

The resulting home closing revenue of $2B represents 2% year-over-year growth. While this growth rate is tepid, it is a relatively strong accomplishment given the slowdown in the housing market, the ability to hold margins flat quarter-over-quarter despite incentives and competition, and increases in cancellation rates from a year ago (9.4% vs 14.6% of gross orders, 5.2% vs 9.2% of beginning backlog). Over the past 2 1/2 years, the company has maintained a tight margin range between 23% and 25%, right at the 25% margin threshold I have claimed is important to analysts rating builders. Yet, the company guided to margins in the “the low to mid-20% range” which matches guidance to a “temporary” margin decline to 22% in Q3 and Q4 as mix shifts to spec sales and incentives increase accordingly.

The company is focused on protecting price, and thus margins and returns, over pushing sales because its lots sit on very valuable land in attractive locations. The company even cautioned “the near-term outlook calls for a more patient growth trajectory as we prioritize capital efficiency and returns over volume in today’s intensely competitive marketplace”.

Demand Dynamics

A 2.6 monthly per community absorption rate was in line with Taylor Morrison’s historical average for the quarter, but the company had higher expectations that comes with springtime optimism. Taylor Morrison could not escape the unseasonably weak spring selling period that saw net orders plunge 12% year-over-year. Competitive pressures, “especially in first and first move-up locations”, caught my eye. Industry-wide incentives on ready-built homes (specs) are applying topline and sales pressures: “The prevalence and depth of these incentives has shifted consumer preferences even among traditionally to-be-built customers towards spec homes as some are willing to trade personalization for the deeper incentives currently available for spec inventory across the industry”.

Taylor Morrison’s share of closings from specs jumped to 65% versus 59% a year ago and 58% in Q1. Spec share of sales jumped to a record 71%. Over the long-term, the company still expects more balance. Still, a higher average price for specs helped Taylor Morrison grow revenue for the quarter despite what the company described as “overall pricing pressure we are competing against, especially on spec sales”.

Taylor Morrison remains optimistic about the imminent future because the company’s surveys suggest that consumers are only holding back on purchases because of concerns about the “overall environment” and not their own financial ability to pay. Still, as Taylor Morrison put it “we do know the buyers want to deal”.

Interestingly, like M/I Homes, Taylor Morrison’s optimism extends to the Florida housing market: “there’s a lot of desire to live in Florida and across the state. So we continue to be quite bullish on Florida”.

Other Guidance

A positive for TMHC’s sustained participation in the bearish to bullish reversal is the company’s reaffirmation of full-year guidance for closing 13,000 to 13,500 homes. The average sales price guidance also increased by $5,000 on the low end to $595,000 to $600,000. Thus the lower-end of revenue guidance increases by $65M.

Balance Sheet

After margins and revenue growth, balance sheet sits prominently in importance for evaluating a home builder, especially during weak business cycles. Taylor Morrison has a decent balance sheet but not as deep as I would like. Cash and equivalents plunged from December, 2024 from $487M to $130M. Total assets still increased slightly as money went into real estate inventory and land deposits. Taylor Morrison acknowledged that “inventory remains slightly elevated compared to targeted levels” so they will prioritize the sale of spec homes to meet “recent customer preferences for quick move-in homes”. Taylor Morrison sounds like it is delicately threading the needle trying to balance margin protection with demand weakness and buyer preferences.

Taylor Morrison also spent cash purchasing $100M in shares with an outstanding authorization of $675M. Assuming the company will not take on debt to finance share repurchases, I expect the share buybacks to slow down, especially with the current appreciation in shares. For all of 2025, the company is targeting $350M in purchases. The company spent $135M in Q1, so that leaves just $115M to spread out over the second half of the year. TMHC has spent $2B repurchasing about 60% of its shares over the last 10 years. The returns to shareholders can be seen in the share price since depths of 2016 and in the earnings/share which broke out and soared higher starting in 2021.

The company’s announcement of a $3B financing facility takes on additional importance in the context of this reduced cash and increased inventory. The financing will support the Yardly build-to-rent business where TMHC hopes to eventually convert renters to owners. CEO Sheryl Palmer explained in the press release “as we target optimal exit strategies for our growing pipeline of Yardly communities, this vehicle enhances our capital flexibility and return expectations”. During the earnings conference call, Erik Heuser, Executive VP & Chief Corporate Operations Officer, added that the company has 35 assets, 13 from a previous joint venture, that are up for a potential move into the facility. Heuser further explained that “it’s akin to a land bank and that we jointly underwrite deals”. Analysts will be looking to see how this facility impacts return on assets and equity.

Relative Performance

The market responded unfavorably to TMHC’s earnings. The stock lost 9.1% in the subsequent two days. Technical support at the 50-day moving average (the red line in the chart above) seemed to slow the selling down just enough to maintain the stock’s grip on the bearish to bullish turnaround. This poor reaction suggests that TMHC’s sustainability in this trade could be tenuous, that is, not as resistant to future bad news as other home builders who enjoyed more favorable post-earnings reactions. I held TMHC through Wednesday’s inflation-inspired euphoria and instead trimmed M/I Homes (MHO) as a part of risk management (in this case locking in some profits).

Still, I checked TMHC’s relative performance as a gut check on my rationale for continuing to hold. TMHC has had varying performance over different timeframes. The stock’s best stretch has been over the last year when it has easily out-performed with a +12% gain while the iShares Home Construction ETF (ITB) lost 3%. I included the three largest components of the index for perspective on the source of the out-performance: Lennar (LEN) (the call spread I bought as part of the bearish to bullish turnaround hit its profit target this week), Pulte Group (PHM), and D.R. Horton (DHI).

Year-to-date (YTD), TMHC lags both PHM and DHI thanks to the outstanding post-earnings performances for each stock. I passed on post-earnings buying opportunities in both stocks when their shares pulled back to strong technical support levels: I already had a full basket of home builders stocks. (Now I have MHO profits to redeploy in another pullback).

I also compared recent free cash flow margin to the big three builders. LEN is a large laggard (perhaps distorted by its recent spin-off). TMHC lags PHM and DHI with a free cash flow margin recovery underway from a low last year. The glass half full says that TMHC has a catch-up opportunity versus DHI and PHM. However, lower margins and competitive pressures suggest free cash flow margin may soften across the industry in the second half before optimism rebuilds for better financials in the 2026 spring selling season.

So, for now, I am keeping TMHC as my leading candidate for the bearish to bullish turn. I find it telling, and encouraging, that TMHC is less than 10% from its all-time high. I will also continue to scan other builders in my universe for signs of accelerating improvements.

Be careful out there!

data.title: Nvidia: The Lone $4T Market-Cap Stock Is Still A Cheap Growth Play
data.description:
Introduction

Nvidia Corporation (NASDAQ:NVDA) is currently the only stock with a market cap exceeding $4 trillion and is well on its way to reaching $5 trillion. The company continues to enjoy the momentum from hyperscalers' non-stop capex spending in expanding their AI infrastructure. Nvidia is also actively looking into new revenue growth opportunities with trillion-dollar potential in areas like Robotic AI and Physical AI. Despite the recent price run-up, NVDA stock has a forward PEG still showing a -23% discount. I reiterate my Strong Buy rating on NVDA and view it as one of my top-pick growth stocks due to both current and future huge AI market opportunities.

Nvidia Key Update

As the title of my previous article in April indicated:

Nvidia: AI Infrastructure Is More Critically Needed Than Ever.

There is more recent evidence suggesting that the key capital spending from the hyperscalers continues to grow at a rapid pace. The bulk of the spending is centered around the AI data centers. According to a recent report, the global data center capex is expected to hit $1.2T in 4 years, growing at a 21% annual rate. The hyperscalers, such as Google, Meta Platforms, etc., will contribute to 50% of it.

The AI infrastructure race, which is already well known to everyone these days, remains a key barometer for the AI technology mega trend and the growth cycle associated with it. The following is the individual update from Q2 reports of hyperscaler companies.

  • Microsoft (MSFT) increased Q4 FY25 capital expenditures to $22.6 billion, a 53% year-over-year surge. It's reaffirmed $80 billion AI capex for FY 2025 is a significant jump from previous years.
  • Amazon (AMZN) is leading in absolute investment volume, with the 2025 capex forecast to be more than $100 billion, up from $83 billion in 2024, mostly for AI infrastructure.
  • Alphabet (Google) in its latest Q2 indicated that Google raised its 2025 capex guidance to $85 billion, an increase of $10 billion from earlier forecasts
  • Meta Platforms (META) has doubled its capital spending year-over-year, citing the AI arms race as the main driver. The capex guidance is raised to $66-$72 billion (from $60-$65 billion), prioritizing AI infrastructure scaling.
  • OpenAI is included here because of its reported 700 million weekly active users. OpenAI is projected to run over one million GPUs in 2025, an increase of 15-fold since 2024. Separately, Oracle is purchasing approximately 400,000 GB200 GPUs for its new data center in Texas, costing around $40 billion. The new AI DC will be leased to OpenAI under a 15-year agreement.

With those numbers mentioned above, it is quite convincing that Nvidia is quite capable of reaching over 201 billion in revenue in 2026, representing the growth of a staggering 54% as shown in the table below.

On the other hand, Nvidia has maintained its killing margin above 70%, which is unprecedented for a hardware company and higher than a lot of software technology companies, such as Microsoft (68.82%).

On the hiring front, Nvidia has over 3000 openings currently listed on LinkedIn. This is a strong signal that the company is very confident about and fully committed to its fast growth and promising prosperity.

Competition Is Still Remote

I believe the current AI infra growth cycle (accelerated computing, a term often referred to by Nvidia's Founder and CEO, Jenson Huang) will be dominated by Nvidia GPUs, which account for 92% of the GPU market share, an increase from 90% half a year ago.

Obviously Advanced Micro Devices (AMD) remains a distant second in the GPU market. On the other hand, the fact that AMD continues to increase revenue rapidly is a good indication of an AI growth cycle, which will continue to be fast-paced, e.g., 21% as shown in the previous sector. Notice that 21% is exactly what AMD has projected for the revenue growth in 2026. Nvidia has a higher growth prediction of 26.8% for 2027, as shown below. It is easy to see that AMD appears very unlikely to gain more chip market share from NVDA in the foreseeable future.

In the market like this, the 3rd or the 4th player will have much less impact. These companies may only survive if they can afford to take the substantial margin hit and be able to maintain the business until the later cycle, when the growth and margin will see a noticeable drop. That's the reason why in the capital-intensive hardware space, we don't typically see too many players.

Some recent report suggests that Chinese technology leader Huawei is developing its own GPU chips, potentially posing a challenge to Nvidia's dominance in the Chinese market. However, many attempts in the semiconductor industry in China have been proven to be unsuccessful and even outright failures for many years, costing billions of dollars. There is no reason to believe some kind of miracle could happen in the GPU market this time. A very recent news report reveals that the Chinese hotshot startup DeepSeek (DEEPSEEK) is experiencing huge performance failures in AI training using Huawei GPU chips. The Chinese may want to limit or even block NVDA's H20 to support their chip industry, and NVDA's China market may not see upside potential from the 13% recorded in 2024.

But the 13% shrinking market share has not caused a problem for NVDA in the past 12 months. I think the Chinese GPU market, once truly in a scale growth mode, will undoubtedly come back to NVDA H20 or something else that is allowed, just like what happened in the past decades because the U.S. technology restrictions have always existed.

The key takeaway is that Nvidia appears to have an unbreakable lock-in being the dominant GPU supplier for many years to come.

Explore the Future Growth in Physical AI

More recently, the market has been focusing on the AI software use cases, such as AI agents. Big stories have been frequently seen from Microsoft's Copilot and Salesforce's Agentforce. However, these are still far from the kind of growth we are expecting, such as the growth rate seen in the AI infrastructure race. Microsoft as a whole grows revenue in the mid-teens, and Salesforce's revenue growth drops below 10%.

Nvidia's latest developments have been focused on advancements in robotics, simulation, foundational models, and Physical AI. I believe the next key growth of the AI revolution will be determined by the deployment of Physical AI, including AI robotics.

From a commercial standpoint, the physical AI products are much easier to quantify and verify in terms of value, safety, and performance, more than the so-called AGI or the pure AI plays based on the AI Models. Take Google's AI as an example, when we look at the YouTube channels, the AI technology is still a long way from doing a decent job in telling what's fake, what are malicious bots, what would be reasonable or insightful content, etc. If it is hard for the (arguably) best AI model from Google to make it right, should we trust the same AI (model) to help our purchase decisions, entertainment decisions, or even investing decisions?

Nvidia is seeing the opportunities in Robotic AI and Physical AI. The company believes that “Computer graphics and AI are converging to fundamentally transform robotics,” and according to one company's executive

“By combining AI reasoning with scalable, physically accurate simulation, we’re enabling developers to build tomorrow’s robots and autonomous vehicles that will transform trillions of dollars in industries.”

So this new "trillions of dollars" market is what NVDA is actively exploring and focusing on. It represents the better part of the future AI growth, in my opinion.

The following shows the current AI "Workloads" offered from Nvidia. One can see that there are 538 devoted to Simulation/Modeling/Design, which is the second highest number, following the most 689 workloads for the Computer Vision/Video Analytics. By comparison, the trending areas like Generative AI and Data Center only have 340 and 189 workloads, respectively.

The above workload distribution shows clearly the big commitment and effort from NVDA in exploring the future AI opportunities.

Still A Cheap Growth Stock

NVDA is still a cheap growth stock if we factor in the growth projection. I typically evaluate growth stocks differently from value stocks. One of my favorite indicators is the forward PEG. I use it as a North Star indicator to value growth stocks. The following is the latest NVDA's valuation metrics from Seeking Alpha.

Notice the forward PEG for NVDA is -23.49%. This is still a pretty significant after being narrowed from -38% at the time of my last NVDA rating in SA. So my current price target for NVDA is simply set to 30% above the current price level. This is a target which will bring the forward PEG to match the Sector Median, assuming all other conditions stay the same. Keep in mind that it is not too much of an ask for the IT sector leader to have a Sector Median valuation in PEG. I believe that NVDA stock will have more upside potential than 30% in the next 12 months if it can deliver great results in the upcoming earnings report in about two weeks.

Many investors may look at the P/E value of 42 and believe NVDA is an expensive stock. Indeed, NVDA's P/E is at a 77% premium over the Sector Median P/E (23). However, I would be more worried if NVDA 's P/E were to stay at the Sector Median level. In other words, if the market did not reward a growth premium for the stock consistently, the company would be removed from my radar for growth convictions. On the other hand, please keep in mind that a high P/E coupled with a high PEG premium could be a recipe for a big price correction.

Risk Analysis

The risk for long-term investors could be the length of the AI growth cycle, which is projected to be generational. The past AI projections had been proven to be off the mark significantly. There were AI nuclear winters in the 1970s and again in the 1980s. Many investors may still remember that hot machine-learning AI technology did not quite take off as expected about ten years ago. Today, many choose to believe that "this time is different", me included. Nevertheless, the AI growth trajectory should be closely watched if there is a significant NVDA investment in your portfolio.

Notice that the GPU demand will peak out at a certain point in the AI growth cycle. The GPU cycle could also be disrupted by new technology, such as quantum computing. The long-term investors should also closely watch these risk factors.

Closing Thoughts

Nvidia Corporation remains one top secular-growth plays thanks to the AI infrastructure build-out, which is expected to continue at over 20% CAGR over the next few years. NVDA stock is reasonably priced at the current level and with a significant PEG discount. The broader equity market, such as the S&P 500, may take a breather after making new all-time highs recently. It would be a potential "buy-the-dip" opportunity for long-term investors who are strong AI believers. I reiterate my Strong Buy rating on NVDA based on the latest AI developments, which still point to strong growth potential ahead.

data.title: OBBB Shakes Up Solar Utility Hierarchy
data.description:
First Solar (FSLR)

Our last coverage of FSLR, our main thesis was that the market fears of an IRA repeal wasn't going to be realized in the worst-case way we believed the market was pricing in, and that Chinese oversupply was a problem that was one that FSLR could overcome with its technology.

While we are more pessimistic about the macro and political overhang compared to our last coverage, we do still believe FSLR is in an advantageous position. The OBBB (One Big Beautiful Bill Act) is shaking the tree, but the worst case did not come true. FSLR is currently on the same pre-OBBB schedule where 45X credits begin ramping down in 2030. Based on management's disclosures and recalculating tax expense, we believe that FSLR would still marginally profitable at 2.1% net income in 2025, even without 45X credits.

Of course, same as any other stock in the sector it is hard to tell how the energy market will shape up beyond 2028, and efforts to degrade the transferability of tax credits (which FSLR makes substantial cash from), the revocation of 45Y credits for solar-plant operators, or 48E credits for construction of solar projects would likely compress margins and change our thesis.

The utility-scale market, which is FSLR's entire business, was not as hard-hit as the residential market was. The credits, 45Y during operation and 48E for construction, that support the utility-scale solar market remain available for projects that begin construction before July 4, 2026, and are placed in service before the end of 2030. We believe this protects much of their backlog, as management has said it ends around 2030, and does include international projects in India.

Relevant for the market, but not FSLR, is that projects that started construction before the end of 2024 and are placed in service by 2028, are also exempt from the FEOC (foreign entity of concern) restrictions on credits. OBBB retains 45X credits, but it now limits “stacking.”

Previously, manufacturers were permitted to claim multiple credits. Irrespective of the facilities the primary and secondary components were made in, so long as the company-made primary component was 'substantial transformed' to a secondary component, even captive sales to subsidiary developers could claim full 45X benefits. New rules are that a manufacturer can only claim multiple credits if those components are produced in the same facility, and at least 65% of the direct-material cost is US-sourced, and sold to a non-affiliate. To the best of our understanding of the current rules, as FSLR’s CdTe absorbers and laminates are produced under one roof, and since it has divested its development business, the company keeps its full per-watt benefit.

This isn't to say there are no headwinds. The increased tariffs on aluminum and steel will likely cause some margin compression, and management has guided to at least some underutilization and logistics costs as a result of front-loading. An additional potential cost pressure is that China has introduced export-restrictions on tellurium (the Te, in CdTe). While FSLR does not source a substantial amount of CdTe from China, China controls 75% of the global supply chain. We believe there will probably be some pricing pressure from non-Chinese firms switching to non-Chinese suppliers. As discussed in our previous coverage, it appears that a major supplier of FSLR’s CdTe comes from a Canadian company called 5N Plus, which handles recycling of Rio Tinto’s runoff from its Copper mines. Currently, it appears that CdTe and the imports required to make it are governed under USMCA, so they are not subject to tariffs so long as the copper itself was not sourced from a non-USMCA country.

As for Asia, around 30.8% of FSLR's capacity is in Vietnam and Malaysia which could pose a threat. Vietnam’s trade deal grants it 20% compared to the initial threatened 46%, though the solar-specific figure is not yet finalized. Malaysia's broad trade duties we expect to be similarly favorable, but that is still in progress, and existing solar duties are 41.6%. We believe that much of this capacity is likely routed to projects in India, though it could face stiffer competition if Chinese capacity no-longer-sellable in America floods India which is a price-sensitive market.

While we are more concerned that the tax benefits may go away within this administration, FSLR appears to be at least marginally profitable without them. Overall, we believe the unit economics are intact and that the core of our thesis is intact.

Canadian Solar (NASDAQ:CSIQ)

In our last coverage, we emphasized our belief that the OBBB would not meaningfully gut the IRA (inflation reduction act) that allows for various tax credits that support solar development. However, it appears that CSIQ is now facing a challenge to its entire US growth strategy.

While headquartered in Canada, the company has an extensive and deeply integrated manufacturing footprint in China and Asia. The majority-owned manufacturing subsidiary, CSI Solar, is listed on the Shanghai Stock Exchange. We believe that CSI Solar will trip FEOC (Foreign Entity of Concern) rules that would prohibit them from getting tax credits, and prevent any US-customers from receiving the previously discussed 45Y and 48E credits.

Test

Threshold

Reaction

SFE (Specified Foreign Entity)

A government enterprise of a foreign country of concern (China, Russia, Iran, North Korea).

Headquartered or organized in a country of concern.

Controlled by a government of a country of concern.

Controlled by an agent of a government of concern (this is broad and subjective).

A single SFE holds 25% or more of board seats, voting rights, or equity.

Cannot claim tax credits, after Dec. 31, 2025.

FIE (Foreign-Influenced Entity)

Prohibited Foreign Entities in aggregate meet or exceed the ownership threshold.

The combined ownership of PFEs is 40% of board seats, voting rights, or equity.

Cannot claim after Dec. 31, 2026.

"Material Assistance" Rule

Products or projects receive a significant percentage of component costs from any PFE.

Required Non-PFE Component Cost %:

2026: >=50%

2028: >=70%

2030: >=85%

Cannot claim after Dec 31. 2025.

CSI Solar is headquartered in China, and would trip the SFE rule by default. CSIQ itself is more difficult to determine, though at the present time we want to emphasize that we do not believe it trips the rules. Overall, if CSIQ avoids the "Material Assistance" rule, the complexity of the corporate structure would be a hurdle for FEOC compliance. The onshoring strategy, over $1.0 billion investment in new factories in Texas and Indiana was predicated on receiving the 45X manufacturing credits. If the supply chain for these factories is deemed non-compliant, the economics of this expansion would likely be severely undermined.

Based on stacking rules, we believe CSIQ will be unable to claim dual credits on modules sold to the Recurrent Energy subsidiary. Selling externally, if their modules are considered FEOC-tainted, external project developers will not be able to claim their own 48E or 45Y credits starting for tax year 2026.

Historically many silicon panel manufacturers, even in the US, source silicon wafers from China given they control over 80% of the global supply chain for silicon wafers. Based on latest annual filings, of the around 31GW wafer capacity, only 5GW is non-Chinese. There is presently no US-wafer production, and we are unsure if using the non-Chinese 5GW to supply US-factories would be able stack credits, as it requires 65% of the direct-material cost to be US-sourced.

While CSIQ has a global footprint in Europe, China, and other markets, we do not believe that this would be enough to offset the loss in US business if CSIQ were to be determined to be non-compliant with FEOC rules. Unlike FSLR, the policy shift adds substantial uncertainty CSIQ's operating model. CSIQ feels less like a technology story and more like a bet on management's ability to execute a difficult reorganization and the whims of the US government.

data.title: Red Cat: Hegseth's Drone Frenzy Hasn't Fully Kicked In - Buy Post Earnings
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Q2 2025 earnings are out for Red Cat Holdings, Inc. (NASDAQ:RCAT), and after listening in on the call and reviewing the release, there is little to no doubt that this quarter was a disappointment. Revenue came in at a big miss, only $3.2 million, not even half of the $11.12 million expected. Earnings per share also missed consensus. Shares plummeted, sliding almost 11% in pre-market.

However, as the call went on, things got increasingly more interesting. Despite the miss in revenue, management was extremely bullish and cited a couple of nice drone-specific catalysts to come in the near-term. Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth is pushing for a massive drone refresh within all areas of the U.S. military. The Army, Navy, Air Force, and Marines, you name it, are set to get a fresh set of rapid scaling drone units. By the first of next month, he wants the units in place, and by the end of next year, he wants all deliveries complete.

Now, Red Cat is actually in a very strong position to capitalize on this push. They hold a very strong cash position. On top of this, their latest partnership with ESAeor sees Black Widow production getting scaled up. The company has also announced that its Teal drones have just earned AS9100 certification. I’ll explain later what that means, but for now, it just gives the military another reason to go with Red Cat drones. And it seems they already have, the company secured a pretty decent-sized contract this past quarter with the army. They're set to deliver 690 Black Widow drones. After the earnings call, the stock made a big recovery, pulling shares back up into the mid $9 range.

Earnings Highlights

Red Cat packed a lot into this quarter. The main talking points from the town hall meeting were, of course, about that major U.S. Army contract they secured. It's a deal tipped to supply up to 690 SRR Black Widow drones. If you remember, the Black Widow is their small unmanned aerial drone designed for short-range reconnaissance. Think backpack-portable, rapid-deploy drones that give soldiers a live view of their surroundings in contested environments. They can operate day or night and stream encrypted video back to the operator, which makes them ideal for scouting ahead of troops.

On top of that, Red Cat’s Teal Drones unit earned AS9100 certification, a pretty big deal in my book. It's the gold standard for aerospace and defense manufacturing quality, which essentially puts them in the same quality club as the big defense names. This past quarter, they also teamed up with ESAero, a California-based aerospace engineering firm, to help scale up Black Widow production and meet future demand. That's a good sign; it tells us the demand for the Black Widow is there moving forward, and Red Cat is exercising new options to get them all built. They’re obviously expecting more orders aside from this most recent Army contracts.

So while drones still remain their core business, they’re starting to branch out into uncrewed surface vessels, basically robotic boats built for surveillance and patrol missions. Management says these moves, paired with recent government budget boosts to come for the SRR program, set them up for a strong 2H25 and a much healthier 2026.

They wrapped up the quarter with a comfy $66.9 million in cash and receivables plus another $21 million in inventory, thanks in large part to raising over $70 million from that previous stock sale and about $14 million in convertible notes. Revenue, however, was a different story. They pulled in $3.2 million, down a lot from last year’s $6.4 million. Expenses also ran high as they ramped up R&D and marketing, leading to a $13.3 million net loss for the quarter or about $0.15 per share. Earnings per share also missed Wall Street's guidance figure by $0.03. In short, they’ve got plenty of fuel in the tank, but it seems they’re burning through cash to gear up for some potentially bigger wins in 2026.

What's in store for 2H25?

The words of Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth are still being spoken about on the earnings call. To be clear, they're pretty significant comments for a drone manufacturer like Red Cat. Hegseth is looking to “unleash U.S. Military drone dominance” per President Trump's Executive Order. He’s virtually scrapped any red tape and just pushed for a massive shift toward cheap, readily available drones. And while Red Cat drones aren't cheap by our means, they are to the U.S. government. We're already seeing the start of this drone push from the latest order going to the army. Hegseth also said bluntly: “Our adversaries collectively produce millions of cheap drones each year,” and that the U.S. must respond with speed and scale by embracing small drones as consumables. Essentially, low-cost, disposable drone systems that every squad can use by 2026.

The memo sets some pretty hard timelines. Timelines that are very intriguing to Red Cat investors like us. What they're aiming for is that by September 1st, 2025, the Army, Navy, Air Force, and Marines must each establish rapid-scaling drone units, and by the end of 2026, each squad to have the equipment. So “every squad” is expected to be equipped with these low-cost drones. That sets up a potential tidal wave of demand in the near future. Remember, if those orders come in between now and the 1st of next month, don’t expect to see the revenue show up until the end of the year. Military contracts such as these are milestone-based, and Red Cat likely won't see the revenue until all drone orders are delivered.

Red Cat’s leadership didn’t just cheer from the sidelines; they doubled down. CEO Jeff Thompson highlighted these policy shifts heavily in his corporate update, stressing they signal the start of a somewhat “breakthrough moment” for Red Cat. Even the CFO, Chris Ericson, pointed to their strong balance sheet and production-readiness as clear advantages should this drone boom hit. So management is fully betting on a drone boom, and I have to say, so am I.

Valuation

As per Seeking Alpha, Red Cat is trading at extremely elevated valuation multiples compared to its sector peers. Forward EV to sales ratio for the stock sits at 10.96, versus the sector median of 3.11, which is a 252% premium. This high ratio suggests the market is paying a steep price for each dollar of future revenue the Red Cat makes. They’re betting on big profits showing up on earnings from these drone contracts. Forward price to sales sits at 11.51 versus the sector median of 3.17, again a hefty premium.

The market is pricing in substantial future growth and market penetration well above industry norms. That's because drone sales look set to ramp up from here on out. For investors, this means upside is highly dependent on the company hitting aggressive growth targets that Hegseth mentioned; if execution falters, the stock could re-rate sharply toward sector averages.

What's Next?

The next big deadline for Red Cat is the September 1st deadline, where they’re hoping to get drone units in place for each military segment. With this on the horizon, we can expect similar drone deals to follow for Red Cat. Remember, they’re not limited to the Black Widow; they hold a nice lineup of drones, each used for a different environment. The revenue miss, while not ideal, should provide more attractive entry points into the stock, and upside should follow on new U.S. government contracts.

data.title: Alibaba: The Cloud Business Is Valued By The Market At Less Than Zero
data.description:
AWS transformed Amazon.com, Inc. (AMZN) from a low-margin e-commerce retailer into a high-margin global tech giant. For years, investors have been betting on Alibaba Cloud to do the same for Alibaba Group Holding, Inc. (NYSE:BABA), only to be disappointed. With cloud growth decelerating and the planned IPO of this business unit becoming a distant reality, it seems fair to question whether the cloud dream has ended for Alibaba investors. As a shareholder myself, I have often wondered how Alibaba Cloud would contribute to the potential gains I expect from BABA stock in the long run. After comparing AWS with Alibaba Cloud, it seems reasonable to conclude that Alibaba Cloud is unlikely to achieve the same scale and size AWS has achieved. However, the good news is that this should not be a dealbreaker. In fact, at a depressed valuation, I believe investors are getting a free call option to Alibaba Cloud when they invest in BABA.

In my previous article, I discussed Alibaba's international business and claimed that this segment is a hidden gem as it is on the right track to achieve profitability fueled by the growth of the AliExpress Choice model and Alibaba's powerful logistics network. In this analysis, I will discuss the hidden appeal of the cloud business.

The Bear Case For Alibaba Cloud

From over 30% a couple of years ago, Alibaba Cloud revenue growth slowed down to just 11% in Fiscal 2025 ended last March. While there was an uptick in growth in Q4 (18% YoY), it was not sufficient to convince Mr. Market that the cloud business is poised for stellar growth. The notable slowdown in cloud growth suggests the market is maturing and that competition is intensifying. The bear case for Alibaba Cloud is centered on this reasoning.

Delving deeper, we find that competition is indeed intensifying in China, which is not an encouraging sign. Compared to AWS which had limited competition in its early days, Alibaba Cloud is battling for market share with a few formidable competitors including Huawei Cloud, Tencent Cloud, and several other regional tech giants. As of Q1, Alibaba had a leading share of the market but to achieve this, the company has had to sacrifice its profitability.

Exhibit 1: Cloud infrastructure services spend in China by vendor

Intense domestic competition caps the profitability potential of Alibaba Cloud. A quick look at cloud margins confirms this. In Fiscal 2025, Cloud Intelligence Group's adjusted EBITA margin came to just 8.9%. Although this is a notable improvement from 5.75% in Fiscal 2024, Alibaba Cloud's profitability pales in comparison to the double-digit operating margins enjoyed by AWS. In fact, AWS accounts for the bulk of Amazon's operating profits whereas the commerce segment still accounts for a lion's share of Alibaba's profits.

If we take a closer look at AWS and Alibaba Cloud, we see some major differences between the two businesses. Because of this, I believe it is unfair to expect Alibaba Cloud to replicate what AWS did for Amazon, but Mr. Market seems focused on this comparison.

Feature AWS Alibaba Cloud Comments
Domestic market Open, capitalist policies that have helped AWS go global from Day 1. Strictly regulated and the state influence is very high. While Alibaba's domestic TAM is massive, the company's competitive advantages will always depend on government policy.
Competition Faced fragmented, slow-moving competition from legacy tech companies in its early days. Faces intense competition from other state-backed Chinese tech companies such as Huawei.

Margins will structurally be lower for Alibaba Cloud compared to AWS.
Global expansion

Seamless expansion into all key regions.

Focused on Southeast Asia and Belt and Road nations. Faces trust/data security issues in the West.

The international opportunity is real but different. It's a regional play, not a global one.
AI advantage Direct access to the best hardware from AI behemoths such as Nvidia Corporation (NVDA).

Restricted access to cutting-edge chips. Must innovate with software/architecture.

This is Alibaba's biggest hurdle. It creates a cap on its ability to truly compete with U.S. hyperscalers on a technical level long term.

Regulatory uncertainties within China and adverse geopolitical developments have also played a part in Mr. Market turning pessimistic toward Alibaba Cloud.

Alibaba Cloud's Pivot To Profitability

Now that we have a grip on the bear case for Alibaba Cloud, it's easier to understand where I differ with Mr. Market. There are a few pillars to my contrarian take on Alibaba Cloud.

  1. The AI-centric pivot.
  2. The increased focus on profitability as opposed to a growth-at-any-cost strategy.
  3. The untapped market opportunity in China.

Let's break down these factors.

The cloud business recorded 18% YoY growth in revenue in Q4 2025, marking an acceleration in growth. According to CEO Eddi Wu, this came on the back of a strong AI product adoption.

Growth was primarily driven by an even faster public cloud revenue growth, including the increasing adoption of AI-related products. In fact, AI-related product revenue has maintained triple digit year-over-year growth for the seventh consecutive quarter.

CEO Wu also conveyed his confidence in strong cloud revenue growth in the next few quarters as well.

Based on the recent performance of the cloud segment and the management remarks, I believe the cloud business is turning a corner aided by the success of Alibaba's AI product suite. One of the core, effective strategies that is being implemented by Alibaba is shifting its focus from the low-margin Infrastructure-as-a-Service model to a Model-as-a-Service model where a lot of emphasis is put on providing high value to customers. This enables Alibaba to charge higher fees as well, paving the way for margin expansion. From a customer perspective, this strategy is built around ModelScope (AI model community) and Alibaba Cloud Bailian (AI platform for enterprises).

Alibaba Cloud's focus on meeting the diverse requirements of a high-value enterprise customer pool, in my opinion, is likely to help the company grow revenue sustainably in the long run. Another point to note is that these enterprise customers often show a very high degree of stickiness, which is good news for Alibaba.

Tongyi Qianwen's open source strategy is also likely to aid Alibaba Cloud's growth as it creates a massive funnel given how it connects enterprises and developers to build on Qwen models.

In addition to the growing focus on AI-centric models, I am encouraged by Alibaba Cloud's strategy shift from a growth-at-any-cost model to a profitability-focused model. Despite aggressive investments, adjusted EBITA improved 72% YoY in Fiscal 2025 as the company booked efficiency gains and benefited from the growth of its public cloud segments. In its latest annual report, Alibaba explicitly states that its cloud computing business will now focus on an AI, public cloud first strategy, which bodes well for profitable long-term growth. I believe Alibaba Cloud is on the cusp of emerging as a higher-quality business compared to the past, which makes the ongoing acceleration in revenue growth an interesting development as it marks a fundamental transformation of the business, not a cyclical event.

Finally, I believe Alibaba is operating in a market with massive untapped potential. As highlighted earlier, Alibaba Cloud is the market leader today, which paves the way for the company to capture a meaningful share of this untapped market opportunity if it plays its cards right. As per Alibaba's estimates, in 2024, China's public cloud service market accounted for just 0.2% of its GDP, which is significantly below the levels seen in the United States. As the tech battle heats up between the U.S. and China, I expect public cloud spending to surge in the next five years led by government support in China. Grand View Research projects the cloud computing market in China to grow at a CAGR of 23% from 2025 to 2030.

Exhibit 2: China cloud computing market growth

In summary, I believe Alibaba Cloud is well-positioned to thrive aided by favorable macroeconomic developments and a strategic pivot within the company. However, the market seems to be focused on the previous deceleration in revenue growth, which, in my opinion, is already in the rearview mirror.

Buy Alibaba, Get Alibaba Cloud For Free

To find the market-implied value of the Alibaba Cloud business, I conducted a sum-of-the-parts valuation given the diversified business profile of the tech giant. All valuation assumptions are listed in the below table.

Business unit/component Fiscal 2025 value Valuation method and multiple Estimated value in USD Comments
Taobao and Tmall Group Adjusted EBITA of $27.04 billion Adjusted EBITA multiple of 8x $216.3 billion A conservative multiple for a profitable yet maturing e-commerce market leader.
Net cash $50.5 billion As reported in the Fiscal 2025 Q4 report $50.5 billion
Ant Group (33% stake) N/A Private market valuation of $78 billion $25.7 billion Based on the implied valuation from its 33% stake.
Cainiao Logistics Revenue of $13.96 billion 0.8x Price/Sales $11.2 billion A conservative P/S multiple for a large-scale, low-margin logistics business.
AIDC (International) $18.23 billion in revenue 1.0x Price/Sales $18.2 billion Conservative multiple for a high growth yet high-capex business that is making losses.
Other segments Loss-making N/A $0 Local Services, Digital Media, etc. are assigned zero value in this conservative SOTP due to negative EBITA.
Sum of the parts value of Alibaba excluding Cloud $321.9 billion

Based on the difference between the calculated sum of the parts value of Alibaba's businesses and the current market capitalization, it seems like the market is assigning a negative value for the Cloud Intelligence group. This also implies that the market is assigning a very high conglomerate discount to Alibaba.

Alibaba Q1 FY2026 Earnings Preview

With Alibaba set to report Q1 FY2026 earnings soon, I am keeping an eye on a few key financial performance metrics. While it would be easy to focus on headline numbers such as revenue growth, I am focusing on the quality of revenue in the upcoming earnings report. For instance, while Customer Management Revenue is likely to see strong growth this quarter, I am focused on understanding the contribution from software service fees and the penetration rates of Quanzhantui as both these segments are expected to help long-term revenue growth momentum for the company. I am also keeping a close eye on the take rate in Customer Management Revenue as an increase in the take rate would be a clear indicator of the growing effectiveness of Alibaba's monetization strategy.

When it comes to the Cloud Intelligence Group, I want to validate whether the expected acceleration in growth supported by AI adoption is materializing. Since the hidden strength of the cloud business is central to my investment thesis, the performance of this segment is something I will watch closely. Any improvement in cloud revenue excluding the contribution from Alibaba-consolidated subsidiaries will be a strong positive signal.

For the AIDC segment, more than growth, I will be focused on understanding the path toward profitability as achieving overall profitability in this segment could meaningfully expand Alibaba's margins.

Risks To Monitor

Investing in Alibaba exposes investors to the implications of rising geopolitical tensions between the U.S. and China. In addition, since this analysis focuses on the cloud business, investors should keep an eye on the intensifying competitive landscape for the cloud business in China as it could threaten the expected margin expansion of this business unit. Further chip export restrictions from the U.S. should also be monitored closely.

Takeaway

Alibaba Cloud may never replicate the success of AWS, but in reality, it doesn't have to do that to add immense value to Alibaba shareholders. The cloud business, which is finally taking a pivotal turn toward profitability, is well-positioned to grow exponentially in the next five years aided by favorable macro developments and the company's renewed focus on profitable growth. Adding a cherry on top, I believe Alibaba's current market value does not accurately reflect this growth potential.

data.title: Intel's Risk/Reward Just Became Irresistible As Uncle Sam Shows Interest (Upgrade)
data.description:
As investors, every so often we come across opportunities where the upside is boundless and the risk appears pretty much capped. In such cases, the most reckless thing to do would be inaction. This is pretty much how I currently feel about Intel Corporation (NASDAQ:INTC).

The last time I covered Intel was when the company had come in the spotlight, due to the appointment of the new incoming CEO, Lip Bu Tan. I had highlighted the potential for a shift in the company's strategic direction, and emphasized that the risks were too substantial to consider a long position in the stock at that time.

Intel is now once again in the public eye, this time, following a Bloomberg report which points out that the Trump Administration may be interested in acquiring a stake in the semiconductor company, which is a very interesting development. This, of course, hints at Intel's potential of being a vital strategic player to the US government in the broader semiconductor value chain, and critical to the country's national security as an advanced computing infrastructure developer.

Interestingly enough, Intel is still yet to show meaningful results under the new management, which is understandable, considering that it has only been a few months since CEO Lip Bu Tan's arrival. The company’s recent quarters still reflect operational and fundamental weakness, including margin pressure, restructuring costs, and delays in key projects.

What we are currently seeing so far, is the initial laying out of a new game plan, which holds a refocused foundry strategy, and the ramp of the 18A process node with Panther Lake as the first showcase, and a transition towards Intel becoming a full stack AI solutions provider.

I believe this renewed gameplan, coupled with the US government's interest in the stock, creates a favorable risk/reward dynamic. In addition to the massive upside potential, I believe that there is a lot of fear baked into the stock's price, which means if the management fails to execute on these ambitious fronts, there may not be a drastic plummet.

As I demonstrate in this analysis, if Intel executes successfully on its gameplan, shareholders could enjoy substantial upside, but if it falls short, the downside risk appears limited.

For these reasons, I am upgrading my stance on Intel to a buy.

A Shift in the Game Plan

As many analysts have highlighted, regarding Intel stock, the company's current position is far from ideal. Following years of overbuilding capacity ahead of demand, execution missteps, and leadership inconsistency, Intel suffers a very apparent lag we see in critical markets like AI systems and high-end servers. These missteps today leave have left the company struggling with compressed margins, underutilized assets, and quite a credibility gap, especially against old time competitors like AMD and Nvidia.

I have highlighted these structural issues in depth, in my previous article on Intel, if anyone is interested in reading more about them. I also discussed the new direction the company is likely to take, with the appointment of the new CEO, Lip Bu-Tan.

Now with a new leadership in place, it is evident that the management is strongly focused on both resetting the company's operational discipline, as well as the business' credibility in the market, relative to its biggest competitors. Interest from the government also validates its potential to play a pivotal role in the broader U.S. semiconductor strategy, particularly in areas of domestic supply chain resilience.

In particular, the focus seems to be seen in three high-promise areas:

1. Revamping the Foundry Strategy

While Intel’s foundry segment accounts for roughly 35% of the company’s revenue today, I see it as being the key to unlocking Intel’s long-term gains in the AI proliferation era. This is the business segment that can very well transform the company from a cyclical chipmaker into a foundational infrastructure player. It would position Intel to capture the manufacturing demand boom driven by AI, hyperscale data centers, and even custom silicon.

Unfortunately, however, Intel's foundry business has been struggling in recent years, as a result of poor management. The most harmful consequences the segment suffered were on the technical side, particularly seen in chronic process node delays.

Previously, Intel would produce chips based on its own anticipations of demand, resulting in a significant overcapacity situation. This caused substantial operational damage to the company, especially in the case of the 10nm node, causing poor yields despite years of delay.

Intel is now committed to shifting to a demand-driven, and customer-oriented capacity model. This means, its fabs will only undergo expansion following signed volume commitments. Similarly, the company is also shutting down projects that do not demonstrate economic and financial viability, while slowing down construction efforts in Ohio to ensure spending better matches customer demand.

This broader set up, of course means Intel will be far better equipped to deliver reliable and high-yield wafers, and ultimately capture greater market share, as a result. Once customers trust Intel’s delivery timelines and quality, they’re going to be more likely to sign long-term contracts with the company, creating recurring, sticky revenue streams. This is how Intel would be aligning itself with real demand, and reducing the risk of overcapacity.

This is a major deal, as the foundry business runs on credibility, and Intel is clearly aiming to rebuild its foundation in this sense. If this strategy ends up paying off for Intel, shareholders can expect a higher level of ROIC, as less wasted CapEx translates more productive assets, and ultimately better margins.

2. Panther Lake Launch on Intel 18A

Panther Lake is the codename for Intel's next-gen Core Ultra Series 3 processors, and is expected to launch later this year. It is not only the first major client processor slated to be built on Intel's new 18A process node, but will also feature new and cutting edge microarchitectures for its CPU cores, such as a neuro processing unit, in addition to performance and efficiency cores.

Intel's leadership views 18A as the foundation for at least the next three generations of Intel products. Interestingly enough, the foundation expands beyond CPUs, and also includes government contracts and advanced packaging work.

In fact, the 18A could potentially play a major role in defense and critical infrastructure applications, which could be a major reason behind the White House's interest in the company. Since this advanced node allows for cutting edge, energy-efficient chips, which would be capable of running sensitive workloads securely, the U.S. government would very understandably want to ensure domestic control over this vital link.

With a direct stake in Intel, the government can mitigate risks associated with reliance on foreign semiconductor manufacturers, all while supporting domestic production of mission-critical computing platforms.

Even beyond the political factor, Panther Lake could massively expand Intel's TAM, extending to giant fabless customers, and not just catering to the company's internal demand. Moreover, these cutting edge nodes would also demonstrate the foundry's real-world capability and actually position Intel as a viable alternative to TSMC. If the leadership does manage to pull this off, this could very well result in the securing of high value contracts from players like Apple, Qualcomm, or NVIDIA. Giant hyperscalers like Nvidia would logically want to diversify their supply base, benefitting a foundry play such as Intel.

3. Going Full Stack on AI

Intel insiders have consistently acknowledged that the company has been weak on the software and systems side. In fact, this was also raised in the company's recent Q2 earnings call. This has mainly been due to the company's strategic focus on selling chips, and primarily the silicon for training workloads. It now seems evident, based on the statements of management, that the company is gearing towards full-stack AI solutions.

CEO Lip Bu Tan stated in the recent earnings call:

Finally, turning to AI. In the past, we approached AI with a traditional silicon and training-centric mindset without a cohesive silicon systems software stack and strategy. While we do need to build and consolidate upon our silicon franchise based upon our x86 CPU and our Xe GPUs, we recognize the need to move up the abstraction stack into system and software. This is the area where Intel has traditionally been weak or entirely absent. But we intend to incubate and grow these important skill sets and capabilities under my leadership.

Personally, this clearly seem to be an opportunity with a massive upside for the company, which could easily see its margins boosted by up to 30%, while also enhancing customer stickiness. Intel would essentially be positioning itself to capture a greater slice of the value chain, and actually build an ecosystem that keeps its customers locked in.

Valuation Assessment

Intel is trading at a market cap of $95 billion, and reports a trailing twelve month [TTM] revenue total of $53 billion. This means the company is trading at a TTM PS ratio of 1.78, which is below the median ratio in the tech sector of 3.13. Similarly, it holds a trailing EV/EBITDA ratio of 14.4, which is somewhat lower than the tech sector median of 17.5.

While these discounts may seem attractive, we should also keep in mind that the stock lags behind the tech sector quite significantly in terms of its profitability margins. For one, the company's TTM gross margins of 33% fall quite short of the tech sector median gross margin of ~50%. In more accessible terms, it's as if the company is selling products for $1 each, which cost it $0.67 to produce. Of course, this shows that the company, at this stage, doesn't have very strong margins owing to a minimal pricing power.

As such, it currently cannot really be described as an effective value creator to its shareholders. This is especially true, considering that the company burnt $20.5 billion in net losses, over the last 12 months. In fact, on a TTM basis, Intel was not even able to return a positive operating income figure, and ended up with a negative $2.7 billion. This picture becomes even more alarming when considering its TTM free cash flow figure of $7.7 billion.

To me, it seems apparent that weakness is already priced into INTC stock. I have already mentioned the company's low PS ratio of 1.78, being below the sector median of 3.13. However, it's interesting to note that Intel's own 5-year historical average for the PS ratio has hovered around the 2.42 zone.

The fact that the PS ratio is near 5-year lows, suggests that there is a lot of fear priced into the stock. The market is effectively pricing Intel as a stagnant, low-profitability semiconductor manufacturer rather than a peer to the more profitable leaders in the space.

The way I see it, this discount actually acts as a built-in cushion for current shareholders. Expectations are already quite low, which means that even modest signs of execution improvement could lead to a disproportionately positive spike.

The opportunity with this pricing is, in my opinion, one that is quite asymmetric. If the company fails to execute on the roadmap it is laying out, it is unlikely for the stock price to plummet too significantly from current levels, as it is already priced for failure. However, if the company delivers even a very moderate degree of progress toward its stated goals, whether it materializes in the form of operational efficiency, topline jumps, or product adoption, the market could quickly re-rate the stock upward. This is likely because it seems that the perceived bar for “positive surprise” is set so low.

This dynamic creates a situation where shareholders don't really lose much, in my opinion. There is very limited downside, and a huge possible upside if the management can pull off on the gameplan it's working towards.

In my opinion, therefore, buying INTC now would be a calculated bet, with the odds being tilted in favor of the shareholders.

As such, I am re-rating my position on INTC to a buy.

Risk to Thesis

One risk to my current thesis on Intel is that the political tailwinds implied in the recent media coverage may actually be overblown. We need to keep in mind that the Bloomberg report mentioned in the introduction to this article is not exactly an official announcement from the White House, and should not exactly be treated as such. Even if such a move is in fact under consideration from the Trump Administration, it could very well be discarded at this early stage, so betting that the company is going to be very vital to the domestic US semiconductor supply chain, and to American national security, could be a premature move.

We must also keep in mind that the political dynamics surrounding Intel’s leadership could complicate any partnership the company forms with the US government. President Trump has very recently voiced his dissatisfaction with CEO Lip Bu Tan, and suggest he resign as CEO, due to alleged ties with the Chinese government. Interestingly enough, the President seemed to have flipped his stance, calling the CEO's career "a success and an amazing story". These mixed political signals are really not conducive to foster a stable, long-term strategic alignment between Intel and the US government. This volatility could even corrode investor confidence in the company, and derail some of the company's broader ambitions.

Investor Takeaway

Intel currently stands at a very interesting position, and offers quite an asymmetric risk/reward dynamic for shareholders. The company has laid out quite an ambitious gameplan which could see its future redefined, which is sort of validated by the US government's interest in the stock.

When you can gain so much if the management succeeds, and not lose too significantly, it makes no sense sitting this one out, to me.

I rate INTC as a buy.

data.title: Fortinet: Thesis Breakage Or Market Exaggeration?
data.description:
On Wednesday, the 6th, Fortinet (NASDAQ:FTNT) presented its Q2 results. The stock fell almost 30% on the day. The advantage of having a company analyzed in depth, so that you truly understand it, is that it allows you to have second-level thoughts and not just be guided by the headlines. I have already talked about Fortinet in several articles.

While there are things we could indeed worry about, I believe that the correction of the stock is excessive, and in this article I will show my reasons for thinking so.

Thesis Summary

As I explained in the first article, The thesis for Fortinet is its great reputation and expertise in specific areas of cybersecurity, accompanied by a highly scalable product portfolio, successful penetration into new market niches like SASE, excellent capital management, and founders with over 20 years of experience in the company and the sector. We are all aware that cybersecurity is a very complicated sector for the generalist investor to understand, which is why in this type of company, the management teams play a critical role in the thesis. Fortunately for Fortinet, this might be its strongest point.

In the end, whenever I review Fortinet's earnings, I always focus on the three key factors I consider crucial: how service sales are evolving compared to product sales, as services have higher margins, which improves returns and the company's recurrence. How cross-selling between products and services is performing and how the hardware cycle is progressing. And finally, how well the management is executing and allocating capital. Source: My previuos article

Q2 Results

At first glance, Fortinet's results were quite good.

It exceeded EPS estimates, was in line with sales, and raised the bottom line of annual billings guidance by $100 million, thanks to a 15% YoY increase in this metric.

On paper, the numbers look pretty good, so this market reaction isn't entirely understandable, but there are a few reasons that could explain it.

The results validate the key narratives from the previous quarter. The firewall renewal cycle appears to be materializing, with product revenue benefiting from “upgrade purchases.”

The platform strategy is proving effective, with 21% year-over-year growth in Unified SASE revenue and 31% in SecOps revenue, outpacing overall company growth.

Growth in deals over $1 million was especially strong, up 51% year-over-year (this is a new metric they shared).

First-quarter concerns about cautious sales and services revenue have been allayed by the strong second-quarter performance.

The year-over-year decline in non-GAAP operating margin (from 35.1% to 33.1%) was expected and is attributed to planned investments in the sales force to drive growth. It remains to be seen what ROIC this ultimately results in, but I have full confidence in the management who founded the company and have been at the helm for 25 years, doing a spectacular job.

Additionally, they noted that costs from recent acquisitions and foreign exchange headwinds contributed to the decline in margins.

Regarding macroeconomic and geopolitical uncertainty, I believe it is diminishing. Although it remains a factor (as reflected in the conservative third-quarter forecast), the upward revision to the billings suggests that management confidence has increased significantly.

Regarding the hardware cycle: The segment has accelerated again YoY and QoQ. Currently, they are between 40% and 50% through the firewall refresh cycle expected for 2026, based on the number of active units remaining.

Refresh activity for the remaining devices is expected to continue over the next six quarters, which provides a clearer timeline for the duration of this tailwind. That is, it should be completed by the end of Q2 2026.

The Services segment slowed for the sixth consecutive quarter, to a growth rate of 14.1%.

Management clarified that the recent slowdown in services revenue was due in part to the comparison with the high deferred revenue balances generated during the high COVID-19 growth period. Growth is now normalizing and more closely aligning with the current revenue pace.

Management strongly rejected the idea that SASE is cannibalizing firewalls, instead framing it as a capability expansion within a “next-generation SASE firewall.” The win/loss analysis shows very few customer churn due to cloud-only migrations.

Reasons For The Stock Drop

After reviewing all these issues, it occurs to me that they could have been penalized for:

  • Raising billings guidance, but not revenue guidance. Billings guidance is a leading indicator of revenue. Cash is received upfront, but sales are recognized as the service is provided. Hence, FCF is always higher than Net Income, and we should never use the P/E ratio to value a company like Fortinet or any software company, because this effect is achieved by having negative working capital.
  • The midpoint of the Q3 revenue guidance was $1.7B, and the market expected $1.71B.
  • Doubts about the ROI from understanding margins (the aforementioned hiring of the sales team).
  • And the biggest negative point, I believe: the comment they made about having completed between 40% and 50% of the renewal cycle and that they still expect activity over the next six quarters.

The firewall renewal cycle is as follows: It is estimated that 650,000 firewalls will reach end-of-service in 2026. In 2027, it will include 350,000 low-end firewalls.

Despite this wave of renewals, management insists that the financial impact is not as large as some expected. These declines in market growth expectations, in a company that seemed to be priced "to perfection," end up materializing in sharp drops in the stock price.

The CFO explained that the units in question were sold 12 to 15 years ago, when the business was 5 to 10 times smaller. Therefore, although there are many devices, they represent a small part of the current business.

In addition, many customers are consolidating equipment (for example, using a more powerful firewall to replace several), which reduces the total number of renewed units.

My Interpretation Of The Problem

The hardware cycle hasn't driven the company's growth in recent years. It started this year, and in fact, by 2024, it had seen a decline in product numbers.

Leaving aside the firewall segment (which has grown less), growth is neither flat nor negative. It would represent, over the two-year timeframe, 5% of 2024 sales annually (and that's if not even a dollar of the estimate is met).

Let me explain. The additional increase in sales expected from this hardware renewal cycle was $400-450 million. Fortinet had $6 billion in sales in 2024. This figure would represent less than 10% over the two-year timeframe (the renewal cycle is expected to end in 2026). The management team implied that this figure was overly optimistic, and I believe this is what has pulled down the stock price. But there is an interpretation to this.

It's not the star segment, just the Trojan horse. Its margins are much lower. It's interesting to sell something physical to build software on top of it. If the cycle moves faster, we could also think that the Services segment could accelerate sooner than expected, which is the company's true thesis.

In this regard, we must not forget that hardware is only an "enabler." That is, the larger the installed base, the more software can be installed on it, the more data will be collected, the more services will improve, and the more upselling there will be.

Furthermore, it was also said that this new hardware could support much more software, increasing incremental sales and upselling of much more recurrent products with much higher margins and returns, and with negative working capital due to subscriptions.

So, on the downside, if this situation is what caused the price drop, which now reflects lower growth compared to the previous price in the company's least profitable segment (products)...

I think the downside would already be reflected in the current share price of $75. I'll share my valuation now. They also acknowledged that the revenue guidance for services was moderated out of caution, although they are confident in future acceleration.

Valuation

Trailing 12-month FCF is $2,068 million (unadjusted for SBC). There have been no changes to the annual CAPEX guidance. They continue to defend their investments in proprietary infrastructure (which slightly compressed gross margin this quarter), but they believe it is the most appropriate for the SASE model they are moving toward. We'll give the benefit of the doubt to a management team that has been demonstrating this for 25 years.

Furthermore, they've repurchased $401 million during the first half of the year, but they still have $1.6 billion available, and they may already be taking advantage. They're very good at repurchasing when it's time.

With this and growth estimates of 15%, with 2% buybacks (perhaps 3% could be better), and lowering the exit multiple from 28X to 25X (I think it's conservative; last week it was at 35X), maintaining the thesis that services will increasingly weigh more, improving margins and returns and deserving a re-rating...

The resulting Fair Value is $85, achieving a 12% IRR. If you run a 10-Year DCF, with a 10% WACC and two growth rates (15% Q1-Q5, 10% Q6-Q10), I get a fair value of $104, to which I would have to add the shareholder yield.

For all these reasos, I rate the stock as a buy

Conclussion

I believe that, at current prices, lower growth in the Products segment is already priced in. Furthermore, thanks to the fact that around 50% of the hardware planned for 2026 has already been deployed, it's possible that there will be stronger-than-expected growth in the Services segment over the next six semesters, which should improve the company's margins. I think the market hasn't noticed this and has focused solely on the lower expected growth in the less profitable segment.

The decline has reached the maximum range of % declines in the last 10 years (-40% from historical highs)

data.title: Roivant Sciences: Vant Model And IMVT-1402 Create A 2025 Catalyst Stack
data.description:
Roivant Sciences Ltd. (NASDAQ:ROIV) is a platform holding company that leverages its unique “Vant” model. Vants are ROIV’s biotech subsidiaries that target specific medical areas. ROIV coordinates strategy, capital allocation, and shared resources across the group. This setup gives ROIV advantages such as risk isolation, capital flexibility, a broad portfolio, and exit optionality whenever it sells its Vants. Similarly, it can IPO its Vants, or establish partnerships between individual Vants and other companies without affecting the rest of its portfolio’s subsidiaries. ROIV's main current subsidiaries include companies such as Immunovant (IMVT) and the privately held Priovant, Pulmovant, and Genevant. And, in my view, the stock still offers compelling value at these levels ahead of its promising Brepocitinib data readouts later this year.

Business Model Validated

Roivant Sciences is a company that applies a “Vant” model, which essentially involves acquiring or forming subsidiary biotech companies called Vants. Each Vant develops medicines in specific therapeutic areas. ROIV was founded back in 2014 by Vivek Ramaswamy and is currently headquartered in New York City, with regional offices in Boston. I previously covered ROIV last December, and since then, I’ve noticed some interesting developments worth discussing.

You see, I think in 2025, ROIV basically validated the rationale behind its next-generation drug IMVT-1402, which belongs to Immunovant. This candidate is part of the FcRn antibody program, with previous drugs like batoclimab that showed that more aggressive lowering of IgG can lead to greater benefits for patients with autoimmune diseases. For context, Batoclimab is indicated for thyroid eye disease (TED), and it is studied in a registrational trial, with top-line results expected in 2H2025. These Batoclimab’s results give IMVT confidence to design potent dosing strategies for IMVT to apply its second-generation FcRn drug: IMVT-1402.

Moreover, ROIV is advancing registrational trials for five indications for IMVT-1402: 1) Graves’ disease (GD), 2) difficult-to-treat rheumatoid arthritis (D2T RA), 3) myasthenia gravis (MG), 4) chronic inflammatory demyelinating polyneuropathy (CIDP), and 5) Sjögren’s Disease (SjD). Each indication is a potentially first-in-class or best-in-class, thanks to the rationale around FcRn inhibition and IgG reduction. IMVT-1402 is also enrolling a new proof-of-concept trial for cutaneous lupus erythematosus (CLE).

Another interesting update is that Priovant Therapeutics, which is a privately held Vant (jointly formed by ROIV and Pfizer), is advancing its candidate Brepocitinib. This is basically a molecule with a convenient oral administration and a mechanism that targets TYK2 and JAK1 pathways for modulating immune responses. Brepocitinib is currently in a Phase 3, registrational trial for dermatomyositis (DM). The study is expected to produce top-line results this year, and if they are positive, the company will file a submission for approval.

It’s worth mentioning that there are no approved therapies for DM. So, ROIV should technically have a commercial head start for treating this debilitating autoimmune disease that affects the skin and muscles. Brepocitinib is also enrolling for a registrational trial for non-infectious uveitis (NIU) and a proof-of-concept study for cutaneous sarcoidosis (CS). And it’s overall a relatively flexible IP, which I believe could grow over time into a very lucrative franchise as well.

Furthermore, ROIV’s Pulmovant is their pulmonary-focused Vant. Their main highlight is developing mosliciguat, with a proof-of-concept study that is currently enrolling. It’s also a potentially first-in-class guanylate cyclase ((sGC)) activator to treat pulmonary hypertension associated with interstitial lung disease (PH-ILD). The drug is delivered via a dry powder inhaler (DPI).

Patent Disputes and ROIV’s Share Repurchase

Aside from that, I’m optimistic about ROIV’s ongoing intellectual property litigation over lipid nanoparticle (LNP) technology. This lawsuit is currently handled through Genevant Sciences (co-owned with Arbutus Biopharma). Essentially, LNPs are a delivery system used in mRNA vaccines. These LNPs are microscopic fat-based particles that protect mRNA and help deliver it into human cells. The litigations claim that Moderna (MRNA), Pfizer (PFE), and BioNTech (BNTX) used ROIV’s patented technology without permission in their COVID-19 mRNA vaccines.

As far as we know, we should see a claim construction ruling from the court later this year in the case with PFE and BNTX. That should help ROIV’s case progress toward pre-trial stages. Likewise, their Moderna case is further along, with a jury trial set for March 2026. Both litigations are high-stakes because the LNP technology helped to generate products with tens of billions in revenue. So, even modest settlements or judgments could result in billions of dollars that could potentially go in compensation towards Genevant (i.e., ROIV).

On top of that, in June 2025, ROIV completed a $1.5 billion share repurchased program that returned significant capital to shareholders. All while funding its drug pipeline across its Vant structure. In my view, this shows management is not only confident in their potential, but also believes the shares still offer compelling value at these levels. It’s also impressive that ROIV has used only its existing cash and still has a strong balance sheet, so these repurchases and R&D progress have been financed without any debt.

Valuation And Risk Analysis

Now, from a valuation perspective, ROIV currently trades at an $8.2 billion market cap. This is slightly cheaper compared to the last time I covered it, when it traded at around $9.1 billion. Moreover, their latest 10-Q report shows their balance sheet holds $1.2 billion in cash and $3.3 billion in marketable securities. That amounts to an impressive $4.5 billion in highly liquid resources they can use to continue their R&D and even acquire more Vants for their portfolio. It also shows that they trade at roughly 1.8 times cash, which is also relatively cheap by itself.

Furthermore, their book value reached $4.8 billion, indicating a P/B of just 1.7. For comparison, their sector’s median P/B is around 2.5, which again denotes ROIV is probably undervalued today.
